response:
## Decision

**Run the full pre-registered grid** (4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, ISSUE-41.md:33-43,68). **Reject** pilot-most-expensive-arm-and-stop-early. Do not substitute a narrower 2-arm run either (Sonnet-solo + Tiered only) — see rationale.

## Rationale

**1. The pilot design is structurally incapable of resolving its own gate.** The pre-registered decision rule (ISSUE-41.md:51) is a *delta* (Tiered vs Sonnet-solo pass rate, ≥ -0.05) **and** a *ratio* (Tiered tokens vs Sonnet-solo, ≤0.7x) — both are comparisons across two specific arms, not a property any single arm can carry alone. Both probes converge on this independently: no matter which arm is deemed "most expensive" (Opus-solo by per-token cost, or Tiered by summed-invocation cost per Comment 3), a pilot-and-check-decidability loop can never fire the gate, because the gate isn't a fact about one arm. This isn't a tuning problem to fix with a smarter pilot order — it's a category error in the proposed design. That alone rules out Option B.

**2. The pre-reg already has a sanctioned adaptive-stopping mechanism, and it isn't this one.** Line 49's sequential-escalation clause ("add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") is the pre-specified interim-look rule the -0.05/0.7x margins were set against. A pre-arm pilot gate would be an *additional*, unaccounted-for interim look layered on top of that — optional-stopping bias that the pre-registration doesn't budget for. If cost containment is the goal, the escalation clause is the correct lever; a bespoke pilot order is not.

**3. Partial-arm execution risks silently deciding the three flagged owner calls.** Comment 3 (line 73) already flags grader independence, hermetic-control carryover, and retry-cap/feedback format as owner calls that need `/decide` before spend — consistent with this repo's "Feedback = PDCA trigger" rule (CLAUDE.md). A pilot that runs before those are settled risks locking in whatever choice happened to be wired for that one arm as the de facto answer for the whole benchmark, without it ever passing through `/decide`. Running the full grid as specified doesn't resolve those three items either, but it doesn't manufacture a false resolution — they stay correctly parked as a separate pre-spend decision.

**4. The apparent cost saving from piloting doesn't survive contact with the pre-reg's own scope.** The assumption probe's Option C (run only Sonnet-solo + Tiered, defer 3-rep Haiku/Opus-solo) looks like a cheaper alternative, but the pre-reg commits to a secondary report — "whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost" (line 51) — which needs 3-rep Opus-solo data, not just the 1-rep pre-screen. So Option C also breaks pre-registered scope for a saving that isn't actually available without cutting a committed deliverable. There's no version of "pilot first, decide whether to keep going" that both respects the pre-registration and saves real budget — the two-arm delta requirement and the secondary-report requirement together already pin the grid at all 4 arms.

## What this doesn't decide
The three owner calls at ISSUE-41.md:73 (grader independence, hermetic-control carryover, retry-cap/feedback format) remain open and gate spend independently of this execution-order call — they need their own `/decide` pass before the tiered-orchestration script is built, per the repo's PDCA-trigger rule.

premortem.response:
Read ISSUE-41.md. Premortem on the execution-design call (full pre-registered grid vs. pilot-most-expensive-arm-and-stop-early), assuming it shipped and failed six months later:

**1. The pilot arm structurally can't trigger the pre-registered stop condition.** The decision rule (line 51) requires *Tiered's* delta vs *Sonnet-solo* (>= -0.05) AND *Tiered's* tokens vs Sonnet-solo (<=0.7x) — a comparison across at least two arms, not a property of any single arm in isolation. If "most expensive arm" means Opus-solo (priciest per-token) or even Tiered alone (priciest per-task in invocation count, per Comment 3's up-to-3-cycle structure), running it first and checking "is the gate already decidable?" is a category error: no single-arm result can satisfy a rule defined as a delta between two arms. Six months later the failure mode is a pilot that burned real budget (Comment 3 estimates ~250-300 invocations for the *full* grid; a Tiered-first pilot alone could consume a large slice of that) while never actually reaching a state where the pre-registered gate could fire, forcing the full grid to run anyway on top of the wasted pilot spend.

**2. Interim peeking inflates error past the pre-set margins.** Comment 1's "Sequential escalation" clause (line 49: "add reps/tasks only where a CI straddles the decision boundary; stop when unambiguous") already licenses adaptive stopping, but nothing in the pre-registration specifies an alpha-spending or boundary-correction scheme for it. Layering a pilot-and-stop-early execution order on top of that clause means the eval-clustered 95% CI (line 47) gets computed at an interim look chosen *because* the arm looked decisive — classic optional-stopping bias. The -0.05 non-inferiority margin and 0.7x cost margin (line 31) were pre-set assuming a fixed-sample or properly-spent-alpha sequential design; a pilot stop that skips that correction means the "unambiguous" call was narrower than advertised. The failure surfaces later as: production tiered-config quality regresses below the -0.05 floor that the pilot had certified as safe.

**3. Grader-independence and other unresolved owner calls get locked in by the partial-arm pilot before they're decided.** Comment 3's "Owner calls before spend" (line 73) flags that grader independence is *unsolvable* with only 3 tiers (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus) and must be explicitly accepted-as-residual or deferred — a judgment call the repo's own process (CLAUDE.md "Feedback = PDCA trigger") says must go through `/decide`, not get resolved implicitly. If a pilot-the-expensive-arm-first design stops the experiment before all three owner calls are settled for the full grid, whichever grading/tooling choice happened to be wired for the pilot arm becomes the de facto answer for the whole benchmark. Six months later, an audit finds the "Sonnet-grades-Sonnet" residual (line 73's own framing) wasn't a deliberated ADR decision at all — it was whatever the pilot run happened to use, and it's now load-bearing for a conclusion nobody consciously signed off on.

assumption.response:
## Options for the execution-design call

**Option A — Run the full pre-registered grid** (4 arms × 8 tasks × 3 reps, ISSUE-41.md:33-43,68).
Load-bearing dependency: the pre-registered decision rule is defined as a *comparison* — delta vs Sonnet-solo AND tokens ≤0.7x Sonnet-solo (ISSUE-41.md:51), plus the secondary "Opus-quality at sub-Opus cost" report (ISSUE-41.md:51) — both need Tiered, Sonnet-solo, and Opus-solo data together.
→ If X = "the decision gate genuinely can't be resolved from fewer than all 4 arms" is false, Option A flips to unneeded — you'd be paying for arms the gate doesn't consume.

**Option B — Pilot the single most expensive arm first, stop early if the gate is already decidable.**
Which arm is "most expensive" is itself unresolved by the text: Opus-solo is priciest per-token (quality ceiling, ISSUE-41.md:36), but Tiered sums tokens across main+worker+validation for up to 3 cycles (ISSUE-41.md:37,55) and could exceed Opus-solo's total. Pick either reading — the dependency is the same shape.
Load-bearing dependency: the decision rule (ISSUE-41.md:51) is a *delta* and a *ratio* between Tiered and Sonnet-solo — structurally uncomputable from one arm's results.
→ If X = "the pre-registered gate can be evaluated from a single arm's data alone" is false, Option B flips back to needing at least the paired arm (Sonnet-solo, plus Opus-solo for the secondary report) before any "already decidable" claim is valid.

**Option C — Run only the two arms the primary rule actually consumes (Sonnet-solo + Tiered), deferring Haiku-solo/Opus-solo 3-rep runs beyond their existing 1-rep pre-screen role** (pre-screen already spec'd at ISSUE-41.md:42,68).
Load-bearing dependency: that Haiku-solo/Opus-solo's 1-rep pre-screen data (already collected regardless, per Comment 1/3) is sufficient, and no further reps of those two arms are needed to answer either the primary rule or the secondary "reaches Opus quality" report (ISSUE-41.md:51).
→ If X = "3-rep Opus-solo/Haiku-solo data isn't needed to make the secondary report or contextualize the baseline" is false, Option C flips to Option A — the full grid is required after all.

Note: the pre-reg's own "sequential escalation" clause (ISSUE-41.md:49) already licenses adding reps/tasks mid-run when a CI straddles the boundary — but that's escalation *within* the full grid, not a stop-before-running-other-arms design, so it doesn't resolve this call on its own.
